narrative: I'm sorry to share this news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 50% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which led to the rejection of your application. This prediction is based on your academic history, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.50, which is below the typical 3.21 average, and your school decile rankings of 1st in both Year 1 and Year 3—much lower than the averages around 5th or 6th decile for most students. Your LSAT score of 40 is actually stronger than the average of 36.7, your family comes from an upper-middle income bracket that's reasonably typical, and as a White male planning full-time enrollment, you align closely with the majority of applicants in those demographics. When the model evaluated your profile, it weighed several factors, with your low Year 1 decile ranking as the single most influential, strongly pushing the prediction toward expecting a bar failure rather than a pass. Right behind that was your low Year 3 decile, which had nearly the same strong pull in the same unfavorable direction. Your family income level came next in influence, also nudging the model toward failure, followed closely by your undergraduate GPA. Even your above-average LSAT score contributed somewhat toward the failure prediction in this context, along with your racial background, gender, and full-time plans playing smaller but still unfavorable roles. Overall, no feature pulled strongly in favor of passing; instead, the top ones reinforced concerns about your past performance. Compared to typical students who get favorable predictions, yours stands out for those bottom-decile rankings and lower GPA, which signal higher risk to the model despite your solid LSAT. Looking ahead, you could realistically retake the LSAT to push it even higher if possible, focus on building stronger academic habits to boost future grades or equivalents, and consider part-time enrollment options that might better suit your study style, as they represent a smaller but potentially advantageous group. Strengthening your overall academic profile through targeted prep courses could also help shift future predictions. While this is disappointing, it's a data-driven call, and with intentional steps, you can improve your bar exam odds and reapply stronger. Hang in there—many students turn things around with persistence.
